commentBody: I didn't even realize the last letter was last. (That is highly unusual, which could explain it.) Now gotta go back and see what I missed.

Very fun as always. Thank you, Zhou and Kevin! And for all you Wordplayers who might've wondered whether Zhou ended up meeting her cross-country crossword friend at the 2026 ACPT, yes! Zhou successfully bullied me into going and I can confirm she's delightful in person.
Even though it was fast, I have to say that it was fun. A nice little trick. I’m glad I looked for the clue that was the helper. It helped. Thanks for a fun time before I hit the rack.

You know how I like to fool around with the unclued entries.

Today’s best image: the POLO player comes galloping down the field, aims the mallet, and smashes it into…a POMELO.
New sport?

What a clever theme! Reminiscent of this year’s (much harder and less pleasant) puzzle 5 at ACPT! 

Thanks for a nice end to my day!
Cute and clever! Loved it! Well done! Just the right level of difficulty for a Thursday. Delightful!

IFIMUST say so, the FACTIS, I found this puzzle quite fun, but was not under pressure because of its relative ease.  The trick was well executed, although it took ME some time to figure out.  I got most of the themed answers through brute force, and only caught on to the trick when filling in the ce in CENT at the very last. 

Speaking of Under Pressure, I now have that great baseline running through my head.  Apologies if I’ve told this story before, but in 1972, as a senior in high school, my friend and I borrowed his parents’ car and drove from Lawrenceville, NJ to Philly (technically Upper Darby) to see BOWIE on the Ziggy Stardust tour.  It opened my eyes and ears to the fact that there were more things in heaven and earth than dreamt of in my (neat little suburban world). Mind blown!
Great theme! Loved this one
Boy did I not get this theme! Easy enough to brute force, at least.

Fun puzzle.  Not overly difficult (“COATI” notwithstanding) but it did take me a little while to understand the theme. I actually got more hung up with “ZONK” instead of “CONK” and had difficulty finding my mistake.

As a Nashvillian, I perked up when I saw [Music City Miracle...]. However, I first filled in STADIUMS, then FOOTBALL, then PLAYOFFS before deleting it all, putting it in the [Kinkajou's cousin] pile, and working on the down clues. I think it took getting BEEF LO MEIN near the very end before figuring out the right answer. (That required getting POMELO from the crosses and reverse engineering the theme because I had SCAM for 10A and couldn't get 11D from that.) Very enjoyable regardless!

I did not understand this theme at all until the end. Part of that was having ScAM instead of SHAM

In re the Midi, you could have won a lot of money off of me betting me that a BARNACLE is a crustacean and not a mollusk
The asterisk clues were annoying.  An inordinate amount of time was spent trying to figure out the tangentially related answers, after about 90% of the puzzle was fairly readily solved.
How can so few people know about kinkajous and coatis? Two of the most appealing creatures on earth!

Another groaner for me. The theme was fine, I suppose (I was an only child who received many non-familial hand-me-downs, making me question the premise as a whole), but the clueing was tricky in a way that unsatisfying,  leaving me with an overwhelming feeling of “meh” upon completion.

Things are finally looking up for Thursdays! I have had few POY contenders in my mind this year, but have have added this one along with last week (and yesterday and Sunday to boot, what a great week).
Not surprised when I saw the byline of two of my favorite constructors, together or apart.
Loved the texts in the comments, and like Zhou would have been daunted by the task of three pairs of words  that had ME in and out with perfect meanings either way (so three pairs is six words but you really need 12 because each has two alternatives. Extra points for difficulty! And these were all smooth and not obvious. Then add to that fun entries in the longer across spots and you have a real winner.
It didn’t take me too long—again, the editors seem to be overcompensating on some fill to make sure it’s doable for everyone—but I still had a ton of fun and some chewy spots. I loved that this week’s mini-animal theme continues with fun clues for COATI and COLT and FAWN. (On the first themer I wondered if we were headed for animal rebuses with (ASP)HALT.) Other inspired clues like DO(UGH)NUT, and misdirected plurals like PENNE and USO. Last, thanks for recognizing that this DAD has certainly my kissed his share of owies in his time in addition to MOM.
Pretty tough for a Wednesday I thought. Had a few moments of irritation. But figuring out the theme made it worth it!

Another creative and fun offering from the couple that brought us “Escape Room”, which was a favorite not only of our columnist, but was also voted Sunday Puzzle of the Year by this community. 

I’m impressed by how the constructors came up with words that created other valid words by the addition or removal of “ME”.  I’m most impressed with BEEF LOIN becoming BEEF LO MEIN and HOMELINESS becoming HOLINESS.

Very clever and well executed.

I need to be more observant. 
Last week?  It was the Ts blacked out on the grid. 
This week. I never realized the starred clues were in the same column. 
I got the ‘revealer’ early. But the trick fell very very late for me. 

Always a bonus when the themers spell actual words.  Constructor level of difficulty goes up a lot, I would imagine. 

Fun, creative, and just enough Thursday to make my brain tingle, but not hurt. 

Thanks constructors!

This was the hardest Thursday I saw in a while.  It was just kind of a miss for me theme-wise.  Take the ME from a top clue and add it to a lower clue.  Well, okay, that's not interesting or clever, it just kind of is.

And I originally added the ME to the top ones as a rebus and thought I had something wrong when it didn't accept them.  Annoying.

But the cluing in general was just kind of annoying today.  So many answers are a huge reach like TIMES UP for 'Test Release'.  ASEA for 'Trawling'?  I had to google trawling and it says it's a fishing term, a fishing term to a generic sea term?  Weak.   CAMIS and COATI I never heard of.  And just general a lot of forced 2-3 word phrases.  Just plain bad fill on top of a mediocre theme.

Great fun! Just the right amount of resistance and challenge for me, and a satisfying pay off. And, yet again, I marvel at the kind of brain that could both think of this theme and pull it off. Thank you!

Regarding 37A....

Somewhere along the line I learned the term spaghetti straps, and ever since I have found them to be perhaps the most alluring and pulchritudinous bit of female fashion.

Oh, to be 65 again....
Really enjoyed this once I got the hang of it, very clever!
I'd have found it more satisfying if each of the paired clues had something to do with each other beyond just the movement of the ME.
Clever and ap​pro​pri​ate​ly on a Thursday unlike that slog this past Sunday.

This husband and wife Dynamic Duo hits the crossword idea-and-execution bullseye yet again!

So impressive.

Thank you, Zhou and Kevin, for both the puzzle and the heartwarming constructor notes.

Your puzzles being POY contenders has become the NORM!

When I see Zhou Zhang’s name on a puzzle I feel a little thrill, I know it’s going to be good. Now the same is true for her collaborations with husband Kevin Curry. 

Really enjoyed this one and can’t help imagining Zhou’s original idea to make the ME a rebus. 

Loved the constructor notes! (I adore my spouse but Zhou and Kevin’s text thread makes me wonder if I married the wrong person…)

Perfect Thursday challenge for me from two of my fave constructors. I originally had NIKE for AVIA until I realized that it belonged elsewhere in the grid. Hand up for ScAM before SHAM. And I had mete before CONK and COAdI/SOd before COATI/SOT. That was my final correction for the gold star. ⭐️ I originally thought the puzzle might have a rebus until I solved the revealer. 
My three kids are spread apart over 16 years and the last was a girl, so they never really had the opportunity for HAND ME DOWNs until my daughter started permanently borrowing her brothers’ t-shirts recently 😆
Cute theme but the clue-ing was way too easy for a Thursday IMO.

This is what I want every time:  a puzzle that's right for the day and one that's fresh and tough enough to be both fun and challenging.  

This dynamic duo's delight in constructing the puzzle makes it sparkle.  Thank you, Zhou and Kevin. (This a perfect way to Curry favor.)
I always truly enjoy a Thursday where I can complete the puzzle and then read the column to understand the theme

If you COME UPON a COATI
It might seem ALOOF, even snotty,
But rarely will you find such HOLINESS
Inside such snouty HOMELINESS.*



*For the record, I think they are adorable, not homely.

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
The bare bones of a Thursday without an abundance of meat to chew on.

A bit of gristle here and there.

2026 remedial clueing, of course.

But at least all the theme components made actual words and phrases and our late-week intelligence was not overly impugned by circles or shades.
Quite possibly my favorite Thursday ever. Brilliant theme. Big aha moment for ME.

Just noticed 48 across and 33 down kind of work too:
Romeo’s response to Juliet asking if the sun’s risen yet?
TI(ME)SUP

Dog’s preference for a leftover bone?
(ME)ATON

Still new at this and so far my favorite Thursday. I figured out the ME part before the DOWN part, so took a while to recognize the top and bottom connections.
Great job with the cross cluing.

I can't wait for the holiday season when I'll use my new word SOT on certain relatives.

This theme revealer brought a smile to my face, as it reminded me of an exchange my wife had several decades ago with our then five-year-old son.  He was used to finding regular additions to his wardrobe that were in fact clothes that his older brother had outgrown.  When my wife asked him whether he would like to have a little sister, she was taken aback when he answered “No” quite forcefully.  My wife asked him why not and he said, “Because then I would have to start wearing girl’s clothes.”

Who liked this? ME!

Was it coincidence or by design that two of the Down MEs shared an M with a ME Across? (The third shares an E with the backwards ME in PRIDE MONTH, but since that’s a time to celebrate nonconformity I’ll allow it.)

Speaking of crossings, the cross of ALDA with SHAM sparked my second M.A.S.H. memory in two days: “Here’s some shampoo. Because we couldn’t find any real poo.”

The puzzle was insanely hard for me in places, but I managed to complete it without lookups. 

I enjoyed the theme - I got the revealer instantly, yet figuring out how to use it to decode the themed entries was not obvious right away. I like it when a Thursday makes me think without the trick being overly convoluted. I also appreciated how all the themed entries made sense, rather than looking like gibberish, which happens on Thursdays, occasionally, and always annoys me. Overall I thought this was a very elegant theme.

My difficulties were cultural, obviously.

Pickle SPEARS is not terminology we use over here. We like our pickles, but we just call them by what's been pickled - grzyby (mushrooms), ogórki (cucumbers), etc. Do your American pickles usually involve vinegar? Over here the most common, pickled food are "ogórki kiszone", brined cucumbers, and they never come with any vinegar. Neither does "kapusta kiszona" (sauerkraut). Mushrooms, however, are pickled with vinegar, as are "korniszony" (that one shouldn't need a translation).

The clue for the NFL thing was a total mystery, and I was (justifiably!) very proud of myself when I figured out the entry.

I was glad crosses revealed most of AVE_NO. The crossing with the unknown media person may have been a natick but thankfully posed no problems in the end: AVE(E)NO and T(E)D was much more likekly than any other option.

I'm no good with clothing terminology, co CAMIS were another obstacle handled by crosses.

Just brilliant, my favourite puzzle for a long while! Took me two sittings to finish, and the "aha" moment halfway through the second was wonderful. Very happy: a super crossword means it's gonna be a super day!
Very fun puzzle! Loved how the 'me's were handed down to a bottom half clue instead of just to the next word down. Well done!

Ah well - this one was beyond me.

I’ll come over to the comments to learn how easy it was 😊

Brilliant puzzle! Impossible until it all fell into place.  

A real hokey-kokey: 

First i left ME in
Then i left ME out
In out in out
Shake it all about

Anyway, there was room for ME in the lower depths — such is life!

At the Vatican team meetings, when someone is pompously pontificating, do they piously proffer this cautious corrective:

“May i remind Your Grace, there is no “ME” in HOLINESS?”

(Anyway, i hope they will from now on!)
Two days in a row of very cool puzzles—nice! I definitely went back and forth on rebus or no rebus, then realized what was happening. Good brain exercises—sorely needed.

I found that ridiculously difficult. Needed all the help I could get here and was still amazed when I got the happy music. Companies, names and the obligatory sports references were my downfall. Not to mention the unknown animal (both clue and answer !) I put Reiki in to start, that’s how ASEA I was. OOF.
Can't HolD ME DOWN.
My biggest struggle was to fit ME into HOLINESS.  Story of my life.  A fun one today (and yesterday too).

I kind of ruined this for myself because after reading Isaac Aronow's column yesterday I solved one of the puzzles he mentioned in the column.  It happened to be Hoang-Kim Vu and Jessica Zetzman's 11/17/2022 Thursday puzzle.  Coincidentally, they're another married constructing duo, and they also used the missing ME string, so I was primed to see that right from the start.  

The added layer of handing ME down was nice, but between my heads up on the theme and some pretty straightforward cluing, this went by way too fast.
I really hate that the new iOS app opens the column IN THE APP and not in the browser. Please fix this.
10A - Scam vs. Sham held ME up.

Two puzzles, for me.

Filling the grid,

then pulling up the revealer
to find the pairs of non-sensical answers
and make sense of them.

Both were breezy and charming.

Side note-

I’m so jealous that some folks have
a co-constructor 
in da howz!

I like my hubby too much
to swap him out, 
so I gotta find a buddy.

Anybody wanna be my constructing cohort for a bit?

I offer:
- Notebooks chock-full of theme ideas and themers 
   ( that may or may not look like a twitchy squirrel 
      was given a pen )
- 14 puzzles on my laptop I need to refine
- A fairly “quirky” but pleasant disposition..?

Maybe..?

Anyone..?

Bueller..?

This puzzle kept me guessing for a good long while, a gift that makes me feel, IMHO, IMHO(ME).  Speaking of being at home, as I’m writing this, my cat Wiley is very comfortably nestled on a WINDOWSILL, making for a lovely meta-moment.

There was a crossword lingo moment too, when I was thinking NIKE for [Adidas competitor] at 2D, and then it turned up later at 32D.  The term for when that happens is “malapop”, invented by Andrea Carla Michaels.

Then came a pair of reaction moments to a single answer – a happy jaw-drop at the terrific FEEL SEEN, and an amazed jaw-drop that it has never been in in a Times crossword before.

Finally came the “Life is good!” moment when the riddle of the theme cracked open.  

Thus, a momentous puzzle, a staccato of lovely pings.  Thank you so much for making this, Zhou and Kevin!
Good one. Tricky but fair
Bowie?

Very clever theme, very well executed. Enjoyed this one quite a lot.

Can only wonder why SHAM/MESS wasn't tweaked by the editorial team to become SHAH/HESS, to remove the non-thematic vertical ME in the NE corner.

A bit quick for a Thursday, but a very nicely-constructed puzzle. Thanks!
I think all us NFL fans enjoyed googling two beloved videos today

This puzzle solved pretty easily for me.  CE(ME)NT was an easy tell for the first part of the play, and the ME dropping into the answer below seemed like something I've seen before. The revealer answer was cute. 

But the real reason for me dropping by is to recommend another, absolute brilliant puzzle front the archives. 4 Dec 2014. Not that difficult, but astonishing. I will not say more. 

Keep the faith.
I was hung up on the revealer for a long time because I had SCAM for 10A.

“Assuming there’s no other option for me” is my primary “bungee jumping necessity”. Not interested in an ankle me down, thank you very much.

 It’s great to live in such scandalous times - the family skeletons are back in fashion!

Fun puzzle.
I was stuck.  Then I removed my rebuses and heard the jingle!

The creativity of these puzzle constructors never ceases to amaze me.    A fantastic Thursday. I couldn't put the bottom half together until I solved the revealer, but after that the bottom half came to me quickly.
7D-Admiral Stockdale.
Clever theme and the fill wasn't too bad either. Took me a little over 7 minutes

Hurray for another Zhou and Kevin romp!!

(My romping was especially joyful after changing ScAM to SHAM... HANDMEDOWN makes so much more sense than cANDMEDOWN and it really helps with where all those MEs are going 😅)

Very nice puzzle.
And to say that on a Thursday means that it's a truly meaningful experience.
Thursdays should be a time for puzzle celebrations, and this one fits the bill.

Not much to say about the puzzle that hasn't already been said, but I'll add that I love that the constructors are married and can work on them together...peacefully and respectfully, I hope.
But one question:  How long are your vacations if you go through the first container of dishwasher pods and need to find the back-ups?
Absolutely did not get it until after I solved it and looked at the article. So, so clever! Thanks for a great puzzle.

FACT IS, a very nice combo is

P
I
N
T
O
S

with

P
I
M
E
N
T
O
S

I was fairly sure that bu**ers wouldn’t be ok in the NYT but then again … The bow tie clue was particularly obtuse for those who call them Farfalle The American handball clue was a bit esoteric. Adidas competitor proper answer is PUMA https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dassler_brothers_feud A very good Thursday
I'm starting to think that the crossword is actually making me dumber.
I cursed throughout, but in the end I “sowhat” admire this puzzle.
A welcome “laugh out loud”.
Zonk and bonk cost me at least five minutes. Fun Thursday!

Thumbs up. A typical slow Thursday start for me, but then finally working out the reveal and finally tumbling to the trick was a huge turning point. That's always a nice touch. And of course a puzzle find today. A Sunday from January 1, 2012 by Patrick Berry with the title: "Addendum." A couple of theme clue and answer examples: "High-mounted window you can't stop looking at?" HYPNOTICTRANSOM "Part of a watch touching the breastbone?" STEMTOSTERNUM "Inhuman group of golfers?" BRUTEFOURSOME And some other theme answers: THELIONSDENIM PIEALAMODEM HEADOFHAREM PARTICLEBOREDOM PIEALAMODEM And there were more. Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=1/1/2012&g=46&d=A ....
I soooo enjoyed that. A perfect amount of grit!

Another fantastic collab!

Way to bring me down without bringing me down.


(Collab, srsly?)
About as perfect a puzzle as I can imagine.  Great theme, great execution, and how about those great bonuses, such as the brilliant clues at 40A and 48A!  Kudos, Zhou and Kevin!
Spent too long asking myself what SO-ME-WHAT meant 🤦🏾‍♂️
Fun puzzle. Is this the first time BEEF LOMEIN was used in the fill?

BEEF LO-MEIN.  Doesn't that sound awful?  Would you use a "choice cut" of beef for such a dish?  No, you certainly wouldn't!

BEEF LO-MEIN is one of the theme answers that falls into place as you make sense of this deliciously intricate and clever puzzle.  I noticed the "ME"s -- some added, some subtracted -- immediately, but initially missed the fact that they were subtracted at the top and added at the bottom.  When I got to HAND-ME-DOWN, I almost did cartwheels.  What an inspired revealer!

And the execution is superb.  How good is HOLINESS to HOMELINESS and BEEF LO-MEIN to BEEF LOIN and COME UPON to COUPON!

This reminds me of Lewis and Jeff's NAME DROPPING puzzle which had the same trick and was brilliant too.
Into my running list for Thursday POY it goes!  Kudos!

Started a brand new Thursday streak. After uncharacteristically getting last Thursday’s theme, I got back to my “normal” and solved entirely on the crosses.

I figure this streak will take me through the summer, and possibly all the way to the new year!

I feel this Thursday brought back the difficulty of thursdays.  I was right at my average.  Did the first couple of passes last night and then slept on it until morning for another go. 

Thanks for a great thinker.  Now it’s time to tackle Thursday.
If pulchritude - “is a formal, often literary noun meaning great physical beauty, elegance, or attractiveness”, how can “lack of pulchritude”  mean “homeliness”??
I’m lucky that the crosses were so easy because I had to read the column to understand the theme.

Great puzzle.

Question for OGs:
AVEENO / AVIA: Natick? Or no?
The tricks keep getting crazier and crazier. WoW good one! Actually my favorite clue was the noisy buggers. I knew right away it was cicadas! More from this great duo constructors, please.

I didn't like this puzzle very much. I found the theme clunky. It did not aid my solving nor make me grin so much as delay my solving and make me frown. don't always solve in some linear across fashion, so getting an answer like "POMELO" didn't make sense even knowing that something was up with the "ME." I thought I was meant to imagine the "ME" sliding down and out of the word. I now get that word is meant as a recipient of the ME from above, but I didn't know that at the time. 
Having written some notes in this space in the past, I feel it necessary to provide responses to questions I strongly suspect will be forthcoming. 
FAQ: 
Q. If you didn't like it, why are you writing in? 
A. A philosophical question. Why does anyone write in? I suppose to seek community and validation. It is not my view that the only sentiments worth sharing in this life are support and glee. 
Q. Would you like me to explain the puzzle, because obviously you didn't really get it?
A. I didn't say I didn't get it, but if you need to explain it, you do you. 
Q. Are you just a hateful grump? 
A. No. 
Q. Do you ever like any of the puzzles? 
A. Yes. 
Q. Isn't this little FAQ of yours a straw-man argument, positing that there are those who object to your not liking the puzzle when in fact no one cares?
A. Very much not. Every single time I've written in to this column, I have received multiple responses effectively asking all of the above. 
Q. Why don't you ever write in saying positive things?
A. I do.

Fun puzzle, very clever theme. 

I do think the clue author is being a little loose with the term "paradox", however, which is kind of ironic if you ask me.
Me loved that

Zhou Zhang and Kevin Curry....let's remember to keep these two separated!  You can see what mischief they get up to when allowed to scheme together!

The clever, never-before-SEEN trick had me ...well, puzzled...and dawned on me slowly.  I had to give up my original idea of what was going on.  I was close to giving up when it occurred to me to give up SCAM and try SPAM and then SHAM....  The clouds parted, bluebirds sang....(well, I may be exaggerating.  This IS Seattle, after all.)

Additonal news:
I was enjoying the walk between our son/DiL's house and our VRBO in early evening....until I tripped on the stubby-stone sidewalk and fell down.  I AM FINE!   My palms and one knee took the brunt of the impact, and a young man rushed to offer help.  (If I'd been 50 years younger....well, maybe 55 yrs.... I might have decided I needed a lot of help!)  But I limped the rest of the way and all is well--just a bit banged up.  Tylenol Xtra is my friend.  You can bet I'll be at Cloud City Coffee on Sunday!
I got the puzzle by figuring out that "me" was to be inserted here and there. That's as legit as any other "theme" explanation for it.

Wait, wait--

W
E
L
C
O
M
E
H
O
(M)
(E)

10D [Funny site of famous Buffalo Bill Center of the West]
C
O

No kidding. I visited Cody a gazillion years ago. There was a museum there called The Museum of Cowboys and Indians. Now it has a different name but I will never forget it--beautiful works of art from both sides.

Do I have the gimmick right?
Hand me down (but not across). Good fun. Would've completed it sooner but as usual had to spend ten miunutes hunting down a typo.

.... HANDMEDOWN and DARKSECRET have the same amount of letters. 

Great puzzle!!
I loved how they stuck NIKE on the east side after it derailed the northwest corner.

Amazing construction! Tricky for the foreigners though.

I saw the trick straight away at SO WHAT and was so pleased with myself! But immediately struggled a bit because of being unfamiliar with coupon clipping (I would call it them vouchers, and it's not a big thing here at supermarkets). 

Pleased again that I knew the meaning of pulchritude, but with HOLINESS? I didn't think of homely at all. I had a small tussle with CEMENT (our pavements are usually tarmac).

I would expect people to bow their heads in prayer, not look up, so I'm not sure why it's different. My trainers are HOKA and I hadn't heard of AVIA. Without the V the big name in skincare was harder, as there are so many, and AVEENA is quite niche here.

Spears of pickles? Not teeny cucumber shapes?

I'm intrigued by the 'Miracle' and 'Immaculate' - your constructor thought it was so obvious but I have no idea what that can be. 

The biggest puzzle is SOT - what is that please?
One of my all time favorite Thursdays. Puzzle of the Year candidate for sure!
Good Thursday puzzle, if not a bit too simple. Thanks.

Ach so! Coincidentally, I just got back from Germany and had a week's worth of puzzles to catch up on. This one took me forever, and I loved every minute! I saw we were giving and taking MEs, but didn't get the revealer until the end (ScAM before SHAM.... Sigh). 
Excellent column and constructor notes today! I hope to see you two back soon. (And thank you for reminding me I need dish pods)
Good fun puzzle; finished slightly below average. I literally groaned out loud at bowtie alternative! Ha ha. Have a good day everyone

This was gritty for me and pushed me to really comb through. I knew I was onto the theme but never quite arrived there and yet solved it anyway. I clued in that cement and cent were related, as well as somewhat and sowhat. The rest I just worked through the crosses and had to trust that the "wrong" downs were part of the trick. One of the first times I've been genuinely surprised by the music.

Fun puzzle with very good clues.

I definitely needed the theme to get some of the answers.

At 34:38, it was a bit below my old Thursday times, but i got my money's worth.

Puzzle idea (probably been done but hey): the downs on the bottom bleed into the downs at the top, and the crosses on the right bleed into the crosses on the left, like a video game where the character goes off screen and then reappears on the opposite side. Anybody seen something like this?

A tricky one for a Brit, some brands I hadn't heard of, and it turns out that homely means the opposite over here.  I also wouldn't make pavement from cement, but rather from concrete or tarmac.

I like the theme but sadly couldn't crack it without some help.

P.S is anyone else unable to open the comments section from the Android app? The button doesn't have any effect.
Isn't this HANDMEUP though?
Where is Mike?

What a fun puzzle, though I knew it would be when I saw it was from Zhou and Kevin! I don't think I've ever met one of their puzzles that I didn't really dig! 

At 3D, I knew I was going to dig this one too, but I was patient, as I always am when I suspect there is a rebus in town. It took me a tad longer than it should have to get the revealer because I had ScAM instead of SHAM and I didn't know the curling answer, though END seemed the most likely, but it kept making me think of cANDleDOWN, so I took it out and finally thought of SHAM. 

How very clever and fun to work this out. Once I cracked it, it did help me finish COMEUPON, as I was struggling a bit around there. 

Regarding kinkajou...  I honestly thought that it must be a Pokémon thing. Didn't matter either way, it was going to have to be gotten on the crosses. (I briefly had COrgI there.)

Anyhow, very fun Thursday!! ☺️
I enjoyed this puzzle if for no other reason than the trick involved the Downs, which I feel are too often second-class citizens in Crosswordland. Thanks for the fun!

Today's poem made from words found in today's puzzle

a/ the truth of the city is in the release of density
from trawling in a middle mass of chances…
a little music of d/ here
a sidewalk jumping game
over and over and over
looking up
counting
a/ one two
one two
one two

Coincidence: CBS Sunday Morning this week showed a new way of making cement from bacteria. It turns out the traditional way of producing it is extremely environmentally costly. A company called Biomasons makes it from calcium carbonate, just like nature does in coral reefs and marble, etc. Here's the report-- https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=n--BQG7rn7U

Well done by the creators.  Thank you.

Well,done! by me.
I was thinking a kinkajou must be something like a Pikachu... but no family relation there (no hand-me-downs)
Whoosh!

Hey, Did I miss the kerfuffle? Barry Ancona got a mention! "Barry Ancona, an avid solver and equally avid Times commenter, didn’t understand what the big deal was. To him, a problem with the grid is a mechanical error — far less worrying than an editorial one. He cited a 2016 mistake that confused Mark Twain’s birthplace with his burial site. Even that, he assured me, wasn’t a “tragedy " https://www.vulture.com/article/new-york-times-crossword-error-reactions.html
You had me at me. Well done ZZ & KC!
This was, in a word, and as others have noted, delightful. I actually found myself exclaiming "Oh wow!" and smiling broadly when I discovered the falling MEs. I love it when that happens.
The donut clue was good and the theme was clever. :)
Delightful!
This theme was so good I got mad. Beautifully done!
I finished this about twice as fast as my average, and don't even know what I did.

Looking through the comments I was apparently spared from the ocean wide divide over the meaning of homely. I had no idea what pulchritude meant so I just had to guess it on crosses and moving ME. Pulchritude is such an ugly word I assumed it would be a nasty personality trait.

I’ve only seen AVIA in this crossword, unless it’s a niche brand I’m surprised it hasn’t made it over here, big sports brands are usually well known here.

I was reminded again that I need to dial it back on sports clues. Spent too long trying to remember which arenas those football games happened in, was there a common team, overtimes or something. No, it was just NFLGAMES.

I never had the lightbulb go off. I finished below average time by getting everything across then got the music. I had to come here for the explanation. On Thursdays  the answers don’t have to make sense. Like Al Davis would say. 
JUST WIN BABY!!
Wow! What a fun, challenging puzzle. It took me all day coming back to it ( life happens), but still finished nearly 5 minutes faster than avg!
Huh?
Great puzzle, although I couldn’t help but think The Immaculate Reception was somehow a Madonna reference. Can you guess I don’t watch much football?
For the Midi, I was a little disappointed the answer wasn’t “Hunny”

Thank you , Zhou and Kevin, for a terrific puzzle. I didn’t get to the revealer until well after I’d figured out what was happening with ME, so it hit like a punchline, the old ONETWO. 

I also really adore the text conversation included in the constructors’ note. The cravings-and-household-logistics portion is what the wife and I like to call “interesting marital conversations.” We like to number them as well. For instance, one of us might opine that an egg sounds nice, and the other might respond that she has an egg sandwich just yesterday. After a pause, one of us observes: “Interesting marital conversation #5,231.” (File this one under FEELSEEN.)
OMG--I LOVED a Thursday puzzle! This is a first for a lonnnngggg time. Clever without being way too clever.

Dang - what a puzzle!   Kinda like a hard deep-tissue massage:  it hurts, but there's a pleasant reward at the end.   This one really did a workout on my vocabulary and pop-culture memory.   Plenty of clever misdirecting clues brought some laughs and head slaps.   And I'm in awe that y'all could fit all this in a grid.   Thanks for the fun - really enjoyed it!
Loved the theme! But some impossible combos for me, notably LUSH/SOT, completely unfamiliar with those meanings. AVIA/AVEENO I somehow got there with guessing, never heard of them.

Ironically, the only entry I had trouble with was 11D, the revealer. I had SCAM instead of SHAM for 10A and sheesh, I just couldn't see it!
Now for my consolation prize, it's coffee time.
Maybe I should have had it BEFORE I finished up the puzzle this morning!

A perfect Thursday (though it was easy to start peeling away the layers of the Thursday mystery onion today. Upon solving I had only semi-grokked the SOmeWHAT and HOmeLINESSes. But there is a lovely balance in this puzzle between put and take. 

The pseudo Chinese "Ah, so!" I know well. The German "Ach so" I had not known, but can't help wondering if that German phrase arose from the "Ah, so!" from Charlie Chan films (and maybe also the resemblance of "ach to "Ich"). Can this be plaused?

@Dan
I suspect the German one is far, far older. At any rate, ach as an interjection goes back to at least Middle High German, though "ach so" is so common I had trouble finding how far back it goes.

@Isabeau
The Scots version is 'och' and it too probably relates to the German one.
@Dan No. Not at all.

@Chris You've never heard a camisole called a cami for short? Even if you haven't in your everyday life, it's a common crossword answer

@Chris

So as always YMMV, but just wanted to point out in case you missed it, the words are legitimate words both with and without ME which amps up the cleverness and difficulty exponentially. I think TIMESUP for “test release” is pretty inspired, personally. Agree ASEA is pretty loose for “trawling” but honestly that should be the level of most Thursday clues but hasn’t been lately, they are supposed to be tricky and this was at least a fresh one for a common entry. Just an opposing view.
@Chris OY, any more complaints? Goodness gracious, GREAT BALLS OF FIRE! Debbie Downer Chris is here.

@Chris when the allotted time for a test is over, the proctor (a good crossword word) will say “TIME’S UP,” thus releasing the test takers from the room. When one is trawling for fish, one is, by definition, ASEA. CAMIS is the abbreviated plural of CAMIsoleS, which have spaghetti straps as in the clue. CAMI appears in the puzzle a lot. There are plenty of crossword animals more obscure than COATI.

If you can’t handle clues and answers like this on a Thursday, perhaps the Times Thursday crossword isn’t for you, but please don’t ask the editors to dumb it down for the rest of us.

Warszojer Jidisze Cajtung (Yidd. ‏ווארשויער יודישע צייטונג‎, Ger. Warschauer Jüdische Zeitung, Pol. Warszawska Gazeta Żydowska) – a Jewish weekly published in Warsaw in the Yiddish language. The weekly was published from 1867 to 1868; it was the first newspaper in Polish territories published in Yiddish.
The founder and editor-in-chief of the newspaper was Hilel Glatsztern. Glatsztern, as a maskil, was opposed to the use of Yiddish; however, by founding the newspaper, he sought to promote Haskalah ideas among the Jewish community, which spoke primarily in that language[2].
The first issue appeared on January 27?/February 8, 1867, with a circulation of 180 copies. The newspaper was written in the so-called “Yiddish-Deutsch” dialect, but Glatsztern sought to remove Germanisms from the publication. The newspaper was aimed at the “common people,” with plans to eventually reach the bourgeoisie, artisans, and merchants. The newspaper published, among other things, reports, government decrees, columns, and letters sent in by readers.

[Continued in reply]

The newspaper’s operations faced obstacles from censorship, but it was well-received by, among others, part of the non-Jewish press; favorable articles about it were published, for example, in the “Dziennik Warszawski”. On the other hand, the authors’ clumsy language was criticized.
The publication was discontinued after 50 issues, on January 11?/January 23, 1868, for financial reasons. The publication led to the emergence of social reportage in Jewish journalism and also fostered among Jews the habit of reading in Yiddish.

@Andrzej
I don't think you've mentioned the paper before. I would certainly have remembered because there is a simple question that I'm not clear about. 
You say that you saw the Zeitung's language as a mix of German and Polish with presumably some Hebrew. 
But what alphabet was it all written in? Yiddish, like Judezmo and other languages of the Jewish  diaspora,  is written in the /Hebrew/ alphabet. Did you read it in Hebrew letters? In German or Polish transcriptions? And what was the original alphabet of publication? Again, I assume Hebrew; your listed versions of the paper's titles include a Hebrew version.

@SBK in TO

I suppose I was not clear enough on this - it was all spelled in the Latin alphabet, with spelling reflecting how all the words would be written down phonetically in Polish - thus "Cajtung": that's what the German "Zeitung" looks like when spelled phonetically in Polish. I can't find any screenshots of the paper online - I saw some photos in Polish history books in the 1990s.

@Andrzej
Aha! So the masthead on the original would have been "Cajtung", not Zeitung (German) or צייטונג‎ (Yiddish, i.e. Hebrew script). Or are you referring to the transliteration of your materials into Polish from a Hebrew-alphabet Yiddish-language original?

@SBK in TO

Of course I'm interested. Żydowski Instytut Historyczny (The Jewish Historical Institute, in Warsaw) has published something on the newspaper - it's referenced on Wikipedia - but apparently they are moving all their resources to a new digital repository and as a result none of it is available at the moment.

@SBK in TO

As I remember it from the 1990s, the original was as I told you above - in the Latin alphabet. The masthead may have been multilingual. I can't for the life of me remember which book exactly I saw the pictures in at the time, and whatever book it was, it's at my family home now rather than at our condo.

@Andrzej,

Nice!

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
@Andrze@SBK

Beginning in the late 19th century and even until today Yiddish was and is not infrequently written in the Latin alphabet, especially among secular and socialist communities a hundred years or more years ago, in places where no Hebrew type existed post-WWII and even as a transliterative aid in modern times to some who wish to learn Yiddish but have no acquaintance with Hebrew or its alphabet.

Andrzej,
To be clear, we in North America use the *noun* pickle to refer to "a small cucumber preserved in vinegar, brine, or a similar solution." But we are happy to pickle lots of other things and call them "pickled *thing*" or sauerkraut or whatever. And I, personally, like my pickles whole, not cut into spears. I save "spears" for asaparagus.
asparagus

@Andrzej I trust you've already learned about pickle spears. In America, pickles generally refer to vinegar-pickled cucumbers that come in bottles, either whole (my preference), sliced into discs (sometimes called "chips"), or sliced the long way into SPEARS. But Americans make a distinction between pickles and "pickling". Pickling got a promotion about ten years ago when people started talking about the health value of fermented foods for the "gut biome". Hipsters took off on the trend, creating home-made sauerkraut, kim chi, and kombucha. Hence, the following clip from the often-amusing show "Portlandia": https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QKJCi6M8-rY
Andrzej, My favorite pickled food! https://www.themediterraneandish.com/pickled-turnips/

@Andrzej

What a great picture of an absolutely pulchritudinous dog!

@Barry Ancona

Oh yeah, my wife and I eat those often, too :D
@Andrzej My dad always used AVEENO for soothing baths, so oddly enough I knew what it was.
@Barry Ancona I always called them slices. I cannot imagine why they would be called spears. They aren't spear-y. Not like, say, asparagus.

@Ms. Billie M. Spaight

Aveeno bath packets contain oatmeal ground into a powder. This is called colloidal oatmeal. Empty a packet into a blender, add water, blend and add the whole mess to a tub of warm water. Great for poison ivy, mosquito bites, sunburn, etc. I wouldn't go into this but it really is a lifesaver for an errant gardener.

@Ms. Billie M. Spaight

I think we just don't have that brand here. I do almost all the shopping in the household and I've never seen it. Like many brands in these puzzles, it's probably strictly American.

hi @Andrzej!

i look for your posts every day. when i'm filling a grid, i sometimes say to myself WWAT (What Will Andrzej Think?) 😆 because of you, i'm much more aware of potential cross-cultural pitfalls and words that might be trickier for non-native English speakers. so thank you for that, and thanks for bringing your perspective to this community!

@zhousephine

Thanks!

@Alex

I actually thought of you poor Brits not understanding HO(ME)LINESS because "homely" in American English is a euphemism for unattractive. And then there's "sidewalk" but I suppose you know what those are. 😊

@Teresa We cope! We get to know the stock answers after a while, because certain trigger words in the clue bring on a Pavlovian response:

Gosling? KEN
Snack? OREO
Chain? IHOP
Addams? ITT
Manning? ELI
Brothers? ALOU
Jeopardy? ALEX
Identity? SSN

I have never seen Barbie, eaten an Oreo, patronized IHOP, watched the Addams Family or baseball or (American) football or Jeopardy, nor do i have an SSN… 

So “sidewalk” and “homeliness” are fairly easy to digest. 

But thanks for thinking of us non-Americans! 

I hope i speak for all when i say: We are delighted to be able to share in the bounty of NYT puzzling! 

And we understand we need to take it as it comes.

@Ιασων

We discussed bow ties and farfalle last time the former appeared in a puzzle on April 4, and I reported from a local supermarket that most of the brands called them "bow ties" or "bowties," even if "farfalle" was also on the box; there was just one fancy brand that only called them "farfalle."  "Bowties" is the usual name for them here.

I can't find what you're referring to in your first sentence, but the word you're censoring isn't that big a deal here.  It means nose pickings, not what the Brits use it to mean.  Still gauche, but not vulgar.

Your "handball" reference also took me a long time.  There's actually a game called handball played internationally which is like soccer using hands (plus a much smaller ball and net) or field hockey or lacrosse minus the sticks.  In the US, especially the northeast, handball is a different game involving hitting a ball against a wall.  What you're referring to, and what we call "football," along with rugby, Gaelic football and all the other codes, including soccer (which, BTW, was coined in the UK, not the US), all derived from one proto-sport whose rules changed with the geography.  All of them are rightly called "football" because they are played on foot, as the commoners did at the time, as opposed to on horseback, which is how the aristocracy liked their sports (polo, fox hunting).

Lastly, why is PUMA the only proper answer?
@Ιασων What American handball clue? I didn't see that. Bow Ties are a kind of pasta in the U.S. Penne is one alternative to them. I wonder if the PUMAs are more popular in Europe.

@Ms. Billie M. Spaight

He’s talking about American football. The Europeans, particularly the Brits, think the only sport properly called “football” is soccer, snicker at our use of “soccer” for that game, even though the Brits coined the term, and call our football by various derogatory terms, like “handball” or “egg ball,” even though handball is a different sport and egg ball is just silly.

@Ms. Billie M. Spaight

@Rich in Atlanta
I suspect that this puzzle's trick is very specific geographically. In a mid-Atlantic or mid-American accent, all those closing syllables are schwas -- ə -- that nonspecific weak but still pronounced vowel. But for many local accidents, some are UMs, some are EMs, and some are OMs.

Ms. Billie M. Spaight,

Yes, but the intended meaning, BEEF LOIN, has appeared once before.
@Charles Nelson Reilly @Steve L so if/when BEEFLOMEIN appears as a clued answer in the future, will it be considered a debut entry, or is this its debut even though it was unclued?

Or what if in a similar puzzle, both the clued (and modified) entry as well as the unclued complete entry had never appeared before. Would they both be considered debut entries or would only one of them be a debut? And if only one, which one?

@Jeb Jones

Good question.  Xwordinfo.com, where all this data comes from, files this entry as BEEF LOIN.  The curator of the site, Jim Horne, who was the original Wordplay author, removes all tricks and goes with the clued meaning when he sorts out tricksy clues.

@Jeb Jones

How many crossword answers can dance on the head of a pin?

@Maks

@Charles Peterson - These entries meet the "proper noun" standard of the original definition of Natick, so I suppose they are valid if you were unaware of them. But I believe the original definition also included the proviso that the entries be "obscure." Neither of these brands is really obscure,eand both are fairly common crosswordese, so I would say not a universal Natick, but certainly a valid personal Natick.

@R.J. Smith

@Steve L Thankfully, I didn't notice that.
@Tristan For a while I had HandSecret and thought it might be Handshake.

@Jane Wheelaghan
SOT is another word for drunkard, and so is LUSH. Surprised you hadn’t seen the former before with all the crosswords you do.

The Immaculate reception and Music City miracle were nicknames for American football plays—I had heard of the first but not the second.

For Homely it appears this means unattractive in America but not the UK.

@Jane Wheelaghan
We would use SOT to refer to a drunkard. Maybe from besot (stupefy), and taking just the end, sot, to make a noun to mean someone who drinks until stupefied?

The miracle and immaculate references are from sports coinages for very specific  football games (Music City Miracle) or even a certain play within a certain game (The Immaculate Reception). Also, I suppose the constructors were tying into the HOLINESS solve (having handed down the ME). 

Our pickles generally come from the jar in slices, or in spears, where the whole cuke is cut lengthwise into quarters.  You might get a single spear (as a portion) alongside your deli sandwich (as opposed to several slices already within the sandwich).

@Jane Wheelaghan, in your defense re the coupon clue, not many people physically "clip" coupons anymore. I can't tell you the last time I even saw a paper coupon except on a paper receipt, perhaps. They tend to be digital now, though the verb clip is still used; it refers to downloading them to a store app or card. 

Don't feel badly about that one!

@SP
Thank you. I can't remember seeing SOT, and LUSH means something nice or lovely to me, as well as the name of a shop selling very scented soaps.

@BobSolo
Thank you. We don't really have deli sandwiches (or delis in the US sense) and 'pickle' covers a wide range of different foods - pickled onion, caramelised onion, beetroot, cauliflower, and if in mustard and turmeric it's called piccalilli. Gherkins usually whole in big jars. Then there's chutney - many varieties.

@Susan E
Thank you. We get sent vouchers through the post if you sign up to a 'loyalty' card from a shop. I think it depends on how much you spend. One supermarket spits out vouchers from the till after you pay!

@Jane Wheelaghan

LUSH can also mean thick in a positive sense, like lush gardens or a lush lawn.

Scott,
Yes, there have been puzzles with "wrap-around" answers. And puzzles with answers outside the grid. And puzzles with answers that read right to left.

@Steve
Surprised there haven’t been more comments about cement, but maybe because that was argued extensively a few weeks back (regarding mortar if I remember correctly?). Concrete would be the best term over here based on how it’s clued, but it’s more of a technical definition (concrete being made of cement, water, and aggregate), and the only time we talk about tarmac is at the airport.  Most people call the large trucks that deliver concrete (for our sidewalks, driveways, and tarmacs) cement mixers, oddly enough.

@Steve L
Adding to your comment, homely and comely are opposites, and it’s interesting (to me, at least), that it sort if echoes the ScAM/SHAM confusion mentioned by many.

@BobSolo--tarmac was invented by a Scotsman named MacAdam and he called it "Tarmacadam", and we now call it Asphalt. Airport runways and taxiways were paved with asphalt, until planes became too heavy, so they switched to concrete, but the word tarmac is still used there.
@Steve I came to see if any civil engineers came to quibble. Cement is part of concrete, but it is just a powder without water and aggregate.

@Steve

Try as I might, my efforts to get folks to use “cement” (the glue that binds gravel, sand, and water to make concrete) and concrete (the material used to pave roads, sidewalks (“pavements” to Brits), etc) correctly seem to be in vain.  The crossword editors should tighten this up IMHO.  

Thank you Steve 😊

Mark

@BobSolo
Not an accident. The Anglo-Saxon roots of English let SH and SK intertwine freely. Most commonly used example: SKirt vs SHirt. Now, in our seamed and furbelowed state, we think of the differences between the two items of clothing. But S*IRT was once just a piece of cloth of any sort to cover the embarrassing and/or chilly nakedness.
@Billy It is a description of what happened, not an instruction of what to do.

@Barry Ancona

If I hadn't seen the alert in the Wordplay column, and if I were someone solved in The Magazine, I would have been amongst those worried about sudden onset dementia, for sure.
So much of solving is based on expectations:
Expectation of success (or failure.) Expectation that there could be a gimmick. And, most definitely, expectation that the grid will match up with the clues...

Mistakes will happen, and I agree it's not a tragedy per se, but it is kind of tragic to find oneself suddenly worried about a possibly serious mental turn!

@Josh
This reminds me of perhaps my favourite joke ever. In brief:
Comedians' convention. After-dinner speakers. The assembly kniws comedy so well, they have numbered all jokes.
Speaker A: "522." (Dead silence.)
Speaker B: "522." (Crickets.)
Speaker C: "522." (The room erupts in laughter. )
Younger comedian in the crowd to elder: "But why are they all laughing? He said exactly the same joke."
Elder: "Yeah but it was the way that he told it."
